title: Refinishing a brass drawer pull
subtitle: Strip tarnish from a brass drawer pull and re-lacquer to a clean finish
excerpt: How to remove dull or pitted lacquer from a brass drawer pull, polish the brass to a clean shine, and re-lacquer so the finish stays bright. Covers gentle stripping, hand polishing, and brush application of a clear lacquer.

glossaryTerms.definition: A clear solvent-based or water-based film coat that seals a polished brass surface from air, slowing the return of tarnish for two to five years.

content.text: Old brass drawer pulls dull as the factory
clear lacquer
breaks down. The brass is still good underneath. Strip, polish, and re-lacquer takes about an hour per set of pulls.
Step 1, remove the pulls
Open the drawer. Hold each pull from the front. Undo the screw or bolt from the back of the drawer face. Set the screws in a small dish so they do not roll away. Stand the pulls on a folded towel.
Step 2, strip the old lacquer

Lay the pulls in a small heat-safe glass or ceramic bowl. Pour just-boiled water over them with a splash of washing-up liquid. Leave for 10 minutes. The old lacquer softens and lifts. Lift each pull out with tongs, rinse, and wipe with a cloth. Any stubborn lacquer comes off with a cotton bud soaked in acetone.
Step 3, polish the brass

Apply brass polish (Brasso or any equivalent metal polish) to a soft cotton cloth. Rub each pull in small circles until the polish darkens with tarnish. Wipe clean with a fresh cloth. For pits in the brass, work with very fine wire wool (grade 0000) along the grain of the brass. Polish again with a clean cloth. The brass should now shine evenly.
Step 4, degrease before lacquering

Wipe each pull with a clean cloth dampened in acetone or methylated spirits. This removes any polish residue and skin oils from handling. Hold the pulls by the shank or the threaded bolt from now on, not by the polished face.
Step 5, brush on the lacquer

Use a small soft brush (a 12 mm artist brush is fine) and a clear metal lacquer. Brush a thin even coat over the polished surface. Allow to dry for 30 minutes. Brush a second thin coat at 90 degrees to the first to even out any brush marks. Allow to dry for 24 hours before refitting.
Step 6, refit the pulls
Hold each pull from the back. Push the bolt through the drawer face. Tighten the screw from inside by hand, then snug with the screwdriver. Do not over-tighten; a brass thread strips easily.
Variations

For an antique pull with patina the owner wants to keep, skip the polish and apply the lacquer over the dark surface. The lacquer seals the patina so it does not darken further. Brass that has been pitted or burned in a fire needs a metal restorer first; ordinary polish will not reach into the pits.
Care

A lacquered pull stays bright for two to five years in normal use. Wipe with a dry cloth weekly. When the lacquer starts to dull or chip, the whole job repeats from step 2. A second strip-and-polish is no harder than the first.
Troubleshooter

attrs.cause: The brass was not fully degreased before lacquering, or the lacquer was applied too thick in one coat.
attrs.fix: Strip the cloudy coat with acetone, degrease again, and apply two thinner coats.

attrs.symptom: Brush marks show through the dried lacquer
attrs.cause: The brush was loaded too heavily or the second coat was applied before the first had set.
attrs.fix: Wait until the surface is dry to a light touch, then brush a thin third coat in a single direction. Let it self-level.

attrs.symptom: The pull tarnishes again within a few months
attrs.cause: The lacquer film has been worn through at the hand-contact point, or a chemical cleaner has stripped the lacquer.
attrs.fix: Touch up the worn spot with one extra brush coat once a year. Avoid bleach and ammonia-based cleaners on lacquered brass.
